cards.definition: the family name shared by members of a family, written after the first name
your first name, the personal name chosen for you at birth
a word placed before your name to show your status, such as Mr, Mrs, Ms, or Dr
the day, month, and year you were born, often written as DD/MM/YYYY
whether you are male, female, or another identity, as asked on official forms
the country you legally belong to as a citizen
whether you are single, married, divorced, or widowed
a woman's family name before she married and took her partner's surname
your closest living relative, the person to contact in an emergency
your job or profession, as written on official documents
your name written in your own handwriting to show agreement or identity
the first letter of a name, often used on forms to mark agreement
a title or word placed before a name, such as Mr, Mrs, or Dr
letters placed after a name, such as Jr, Sr, or III
another name that a person is known by, either officially or informally
a name given to you at birth that comes between your first name and surname
the city or town where you were born, as recorded on official documents
any name you used in the past before it was officially changed
a document that shows who you are, such as a passport or driving licence
your basic information such as name, address, and date of birth
the fixed address where you normally live, not a temporary one
an address where you are staying for a short time only
the group of letters and numbers added to an address to help sort mail
a telephone connected by wire to a fixed location, not a mobile phone
a phone you carry with you, not connected to a fixed line
the address used to send and receive electronic messages
a person to call if something serious happens to you
a new address where your mail should be sent after you move
the place where someone lives, especially as used on official forms
used in an address to show the letter should go through another person
a region or area within a country, sometimes needed on address forms
a person who pays rent to live in a property they do not own
the owner of a property who rents it to someone else
any person who currently lives at or uses a property
all the people who live together at the same address
a regular bill for services like gas, electricity, or water at your home
a document that shows where you live, such as a bank statement or utility bill
the address where you want letters and official mail to be sent
the official address on record for a person or business
a numbered box at a post office where your mail is kept for you to collect
officially given or sent out by an authority, such as a government or company
the date after which a document or product is no longer accepted or safe to use
a unique code given to track a document, application, or transaction
a unique number printed on a product or document to identify it
an example used to show what something looks like, especially a sample signature
an exact copy of something, especially a document or key
the first and genuine version of a document, not a copy
officially confirmed as true or correct, usually stamped and signed
to sign a document that someone else has already signed, to confirm it
a person who watches you sign a document and then signs it too to confirm it happened
to have an official person confirm that a document or signature is genuine
to formally state or confirm that something is true or genuine
clear enough to be read, especially handwriting or printed text
a paper copy of a document made by a machine
officially promised to be true, often in a legal or formal setting
a paper or email that proves you paid for something
an official legal document, especially one that proves ownership of property
a statement that says someone is not responsible for certain risks or outcomes
extra information added at the end of a document or form
the rules you agree to when you sign up for a service or product
write in clear separate letters, not in joined-up handwriting
large separate letters written without joining, like A B C
a small check mark used on forms to show a choice or confirm something
to draw a line through something written to show it is wrong or removed
to draw a round shape around something to highlight or select it
cross out the options that do not apply to you, leaving only the correct one
look at the other side of this page for more information
fill in every part of the form with no sections left empty
do not write anything in this space
this question or section does not apply to your situation
short for 'not applicable,' used when a question does not apply to you
to put something inside an envelope or package along with a letter
to fasten or join something to a form, letter, or email
to keep something for yourself and not give it away or throw it out
to tear or cut a part off from the rest of a document
to draw a line under a word or phrase to highlight it
to mark something so it stands out, using colour or bold text
written with a pen, not a pencil, so it cannot be erased
in the specific place marked or pointed out on the form
the same as what was written in the section before this one
having a paid job, working for an employer
not currently working in a paid job but looking for one
working for yourself rather than for a company or employer
no longer working because of age, having finished your working life
a person who is studying at a school, college, or university
a person who relies on you for money and support, such as a child
a person named to receive money or benefits, such as from insurance or a will
a person who is applying for something, such as a job or a visa
a person who is asking for money or benefits they believe they are owed
a person chosen or named by someone else, often to act on their behalf
a person who promises to pay if someone else cannot, such as for rent
the person you are married to, your husband or wife
a person legally responsible for a child who is not their own
the person named in a will to carry out the wishes of the person who died
a person given legal control of money or property for someone else's benefit
a person receiving a pension, usually someone who has retired from work
a person who looks after someone who is ill, elderly, or has a disability
a person who runs a business alone and is fully responsible for it
a person who owns the home they live in rather than renting it
a woman whose husband or partner has died and who has not married again
a word used at the start of a letter or email before the person's name
a formal way to end a letter when you know the person's name
a formal way to end a letter when you do not know the person's name
a polite but slightly formal way to end an email or letter
a warm and polite way to end an email, slightly softer than 'regards'
a friendly way to end a letter or email, less formal than regards
a very formal greeting used when you do not know who will read the letter
a formal greeting used in letters when you do not know the reader's name
a polite phrase used when sending something officially, often on a slip of paper
a very warm and friendly way to end a letter or email
a formal and polite way to end a letter, showing high regard for the reader
a friendly way to end an email when you are thanking someone for their help

cards.TargetWord: looking forward to hearing from you
cards.definition: a polite closing phrase that shows you expect a reply
a formal phrase meaning the documents mentioned are included with this letter
a standard opening phrase in formal letters and emails that states your purpose
a friendly, informal way to say goodbye at the end of a message
a request to reply and say whether you will attend an event
to say yes to an invitation, offer, or request
to politely say no to an invitation, offer, or request
a formal phrase used to deliver bad news politely in a letter or email
to go to an event, meeting, or appointment
an official message or document that proves something has been agreed or booked
a message that confirms something has been received or noticed
acting or speaking for someone else, representing another person
done to show respect or celebrate a particular person or event
in a warm and polite way, often used on formal invitations
to move an event to a later date or time
the place where an event, meeting, or activity will happen
a formal social event held to welcome guests or celebrate something
the rules about what type of clothing is expected at an event or workplace
an extra person you are allowed to bring with you to an event
an early notice asking you to keep a particular day free for an event
a special event or a particular time when something happens
a written or spoken request asking someone to come to an event
a polite way of making a request, softer than 'please'
done quickly and without delay
following on from something already discussed, such as a phone call or email
with this letter or email, used when something is included
included inside the envelope along with the letter
added to an email as a separate file, such as a document or photo
about a particular subject or topic
about or relating to a particular matter
to be thankful for something, or to understand its value
to say you are sorry for something that has happened
a problem or difficulty that causes trouble, even if small
as soon as you are able to, without being too demanding
a polite way of saying 'feel free to contact us if you need to'
a polite way to make a request in a formal letter or email
a phrase used when sharing something useful that may not need a reply
referring to something that was already talked about, usually in a meeting or call
about or in connection with a particular matter or document
a polite way of asking someone to give their opinion or instructions
a short reply meaning 'I have read and understood your message'
according to or in line with something already agreed or stated
to check on something again or continue a conversation after a delay
a question, especially one asked to get information or clarify something
the short title at the top of an email that tells the reader what it is about
a copy of an email sent to someone who is not the main reader
a hidden copy of an email where other readers cannot see the name
to send an email you received to another person

the main folder in your email where new messages arrive
unwanted emails sent in bulk, often advertising or scams
to remove yourself from a mailing list so you stop receiving emails
the person who sent the email or message
the person who receives an email, letter, or message
an automatic reply that tells people you are away and cannot respond
a notification that tells the sender when their email has been opened
when an email is returned to you because it could not be delivered
to mark an email as important so you can find it again easily
to move an email out of your inbox without deleting it, for storage

a warning to be careful because there may be danger nearby

limited or controlled, only allowed for certain people or at certain times

people who enter private land or property without permission

broken and not working, temporarily unable to be used

questions or requests for information, or the place where you ask them

do not stand, park, or place anything in this area

wait in line at this spot until it is your turn

shut temporarily for repairs or cleaning, not open to the public
not moving, staying in one place
separate and distinct, not connected to each other

the noun form meaning the act of doing something regularly, or a business
a suggestion or recommendation about what someone should do
a large, dry area of land with very little rain or plant life
to leave your own country to go and live in another one permanently
relating to what is right and wrong, or a lesson learned from a story
to come before something else in time, order, or position
to draw out a response, reaction, or information from someone

having a strong dislike or opposition to something
brought into the world, the start of a person's life
rough in texture, or rude and lacking in manners
to make certain that something will happen or be the case
next to, at the side of someone or something
the narrow part of the body between the ribs and the hips
having two parts, functions, or aspects

used to introduce two possibilities or alternatives

belonging to a thing, animal, or idea already mentioned
in that place, or used to introduce that something exists
belonging to the person being spoken or written to
used to compare two things or show a difference between them
not tight, not firmly fixed in place

to put something down flat, or the past tense of 'lie'
used as the subject of a verb when asking about or referring to a person
used to ask for or give permission, or to say something is possible
put to death by hanging, the only correct past tense for this meaning

at or to a greater physical distance from a point

to show off something you are proud of in an obvious way
confused and puzzled, not sure what is happening or what to think
no longer needed, or in British English, dismissed from a job because the role ended
the great evil or seriousness of something, not just its large size
feeling sick in the stomach, as though you might vomit
to switch back and forth between two things in turn

to make an existing problem or condition worse than it already was

happening by chance or accident, not necessarily a good thing

busy or occupied, or having agreed to marry someone

extremely good, wonderful

one thousand million, the number 1,000,000,000

useful, convenient, or easy to use when you need it

not joined or connected, or to divide things into different parts

to measure or estimate something, or a device used for measuring

playfully naughty or causing minor trouble in a harmless way

a period of one thousand years
strange, unusual, or difficult to explain
the work of keeping something in good condition through regular care and repair

to spell a word incorrectly

in addition, used to introduce an extra point that strengthens the argument
as an extra thing, on top of what has already been mentioned
in addition to something, or apart from something already mentioned
as well as what has already been said or done
to the same degree, or used to introduce a point of equal importance
in a similar way, used to draw a comparison between two things
in the same way, also used to mean 'the same to you'
used with 'but also' to show that two things are both true
in addition to, together with something else already mentioned
together with, in company with something or someone else
combined with, when two things together create an effect
used to add a point that is even more important or surprising
for the same reason, following the same logic as the previous point
in addition to everything else, used to add one more thing to a list
most importantly, more than anything else
without even mentioning, used to add something obvious that strengthens the point
used to say that if the first thing is unlikely, the second is even more so
that is to say, used to give specific details or names
in addition, as well, used to add another point or fact
despite that, used to introduce a point that contrasts with what came before
despite the fact that, used to introduce a contrasting point within the same sentence
in spite of that, used to show that something is true despite what was just said
while on the other hand, used to show a clear difference between two things
without being affected by, even though something is the case
used to present a contrasting point of view or alternative consideration
in the opposite way, used to show the reverse of what was just said
despite that, but at the same time, or up until now
despite what has been said, continuing to be the case
used to accept that something is true before introducing a contrasting point
despite that, used to acknowledge something and still present a different view
despite what has just been said, it does not change the outcome
used after making a point to introduce a contrasting or qualifying thought
even if that is true, used to acknowledge a point before dismissing it
used to show a clear difference between two things being compared
used to say that the opposite of what was just said is actually true
used to accept that something is true, often before making a contrasting point
in spite of what has just been said, another form of nevertheless
without being affected by anything else, no matter what
without being prevented by, even though something is working against it

as a result, because of what happened before
in a way that is suitable for the situation, or as a result

in this way, or as a result of this
